summary: codebuff-cli typosquats CodebuffAI's `codebuff` and ships a third-party binary dropper with TLS verification disabled
details: Package `codebuff-cli` impersonates the legitimate `codebuff` package by CodebuffAI. The README copies CodebuffAI's marketing verbatim and even instructs users to `npm install -g codebuff`, but the actual bin script (`cli/bin/codebuff.cjs`) downloads a native binary from a personal GitHub account (`Marcus-Mok-GH/codebuff-cli`) unrelated to the real publisher. The fetch targets the mutable `latest` release tag with no hash or signature verification, chmods the result to 0755, and spawns it — the canonical generic-binary-runner-dropper shape (mutable URL + publisher mismatch + no integrity check + immediate exec). The spawned child is launched with `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0` injected into its environment, globally disabling TLS certificate validation for every HTTPS call the binary makes; there is no legitimate reason for an AI coding CLI to weaken its own transport security. README also documents a default backend at `fireworks-api-backend.vercel.app` — an anonymous third-party Vercel deployment, not CodebuffAI infrastructure — so any prompts and source-code context handled by the CLI are routed through the impersonator's server over a TLS-stripped channel. Installers running `npm i -g codebuff-cli` (a plausible mistype of `codebuff`) get arbitrary native code from a non-publisher account executed on their machine, with their source code subsequently flowing to an attacker-controlled endpoint.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
